one_line_takeaway: OPSA is a threshold-agnostic white-box attack that inflates conformal prediction-set size to attack uncertainty rather than accuracy, and its paired defense OPSA-AT (conformal adversarial training with exact per-batch quantile calibration) yields the tightest prediction sets while holding ~90% coverage across CIFAR-10/100 and multiple attacks.
key_finding: On CIFAR-10 (Table 1), evaluated with calibration+test both under OPSA attack, OPSA-AT yields the smallest (best) prediction-set size across attacks while holding coverage at ~89-90%. Under the OPSA attack column, OPSA-AT Size = 3.11 vs BETA-AT 7.34, FGSM-AT 7.29, MART 5.40, PGD10-AT 4.50, TRADES 4.02, and OPSA-ST 3.37 -- OPSA-AT is the tightest. On clean inputs OPSA-AT Size = 1.24, also the smallest (vs MART 1.29, TRADES 1.51, BETA-AT 7.06). As an ATTACK, OPSA induces the largest set size among attacks on non-OPSA-trained defenses: e.g. on the FGSM-AT defense (CIFAR-10) OPSA drives Size to 7.29, exceeding PGD40 (7.07) and PGD10 (7.06). On CIFAR-100 (Table 2) the pattern amplifies: under OPSA attack, OPSA-AT Size = 32.07 vs BETA-AT 75.25, FGSM-AT 65.56, PGD10-AT 39.23, TRADES 32.30, MART 35.38; and on clean data OPSA-AT gives 8.41 vs BETA-AT's 73.65. OPSA-AT also achieves consistently low SSCV (e.g. 0.03-0.06 on CIFAR-100), indicating stable size-conditional coverage, whereas BETA-AT's SSCV blows up (e.g. 0.90 under APGD/Auto on CIFAR-100). Net: OPSA is the strongest set-size-inflating attack against ordinary defenses, and OPSA-AT is the most efficient (tightest set) defense, generalizing beyond OPSA to FGSM/PGD/BETA/Square/APGD/Auto attacks.
core_contribution: The paper (an ICML 2025 / PMLR 267 work titled 'OPSA: Hard Quantile Framework for Adversarial Robustness') contributes a matched attack/defense pair for conformal prediction (CP) under adversarial perturbation. (1) OPSA (Optimal Size Attack): a differentiable, gradient-based attack whose objective is not to flip the top-1 label but to maximize the CP prediction-set SIZE (i.e., model uncertainty / inefficiency) at ANY significance level. It does this by maximizing a 'soft set size' M_T(x+eps; f, f_y(x+eps)) = sum_k sigma((f_k(x+eps) - f_y(x+eps))/T), using the perturbed true-class score as the internal reference threshold, so the attacker never needs to know the defender's alpha or operational threshold tau. (2) OPSA-AT: an adversarial-training defense that folds OPSA into a conformal-training paradigm, reformulating robust CP training as a bi-level / bi-objective problem: within each mini-batch the data is split into a training subset (used to craft OPSA perturbations and minimize set size) and a calibration subset (used to compute an exact THR quantile threshold tau), optimizing a total loss L_total = L_class + lambda*M_T2. The authors frame their reformulation as attack-agnostic, contrasting it with prior CP-robustness work (Gendler et al. ARCP, Ghosh et al. PRCP, Yan et al. RCT, Liu et al. AT-UR) which they cite as either enlarging prediction sets to preserve coverage or being tied to specific attacks.
threat_model: White-box evasion attacker with gradient access to the classifier f. The perturbation is L-infinity norm-bounded to a budget r = 8/255 (0.03) and clipped to the valid image range [0,1]^d, i.e. imperceptible. The attacker's GOAL differs from a standard misclassification attack: rather than changing the predicted label, the attacker maximizes the expected conformal prediction-set size E[|Gamma(f(x+eps), tau)|], degrading the informativeness/efficiency of the uncertainty-quantified predictions. Crucially the attacker is assumed NOT to know the defender's significance level alpha (hence not tau), which is why the objective is threshold-free. The evaluated deployment scenario is deliberately stringent: both the CP calibration set and the test set are OPSA-perturbed (while exchangeability between them is preserved, keeping CP's marginal coverage guarantee valid). Data poisoning is cited as related but is not the paper's own threat model; OPSA is a test-time evasion attack.
limitations: The authors themselves flag the experimental setup as 'relatively limited.' (1) Only ResNet34/ResNet50 backbones are tested; they defer PreAct-ResNet to future work. (2) Due to hardware limits, conformal-training mini-batch sizes were 64 (CIFAR-10) / 200 (CIFAR-100), whereas their own theory (Remark 2) argues optimal exchangeability approximation needs subsets 5-10x the class count, i.e. 500-1000, so reported numbers may be below the method's ceiling. (3) Only three vision datasets (CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, mini-ImageNet) and a single L-infinity budget (8/255) / alpha (10%) are evaluated in the main tables; mini-ImageNet results, per-attack compute overhead, accuracy tables, and a T1 ablation are relegated to appendices (C/B/D/E) not in the main body. (4) The threat model assumes a white-box gradient-accessible classifier; transfer/black-box robustness of OPSA-AT beyond the query-based Square attack is not deeply probed. (5) The Impact Statement is boilerplate ('no consequences we feel must be highlighted'), giving no dedicated dual-use discussion despite releasing a novel attack.

datasets_used: mini-ImageNet (ResNet50 backbone; detailed results in Appendix C)
quantitative_results: CIFAR-10, under OPSA attack: OPSA-AT prediction-set Size = 3.11 (smallest) vs OPSA-ST 3.37, TRADES 4.02, PGD10-AT 4.50, MART 5.40, FGSM-AT 7.29, BETA-AT 7.34
CIFAR-10, clean inputs: OPSA-AT Size = 1.24 (smallest) vs MART 1.29, OPSA-ST 1.30, TRADES 1.51, FGSM-AT 2.00, PGD10-AT 4.15, BETA-AT 7.06
CIFAR-10, OPSA as attack on FGSM-AT defense: Size = 7.29, larger than PGD40 (7.07) and PGD10 (7.06) on the same defense
CIFAR-100, under OPSA attack: OPSA-AT Size = 32.07 (smallest) vs TRADES 32.30, MART 35.38, PGD10-AT 39.23, FGSM-AT 65.56, BETA-AT 75.25
CIFAR-100, clean inputs: OPSA-AT Size = 8.41 vs MART 8.65, TRADES 8.81, FGSM-AT 13.47, PGD10-AT 32.93, BETA-AT 73.65
Coverage held near the 1-alpha = 90% target across all methods (roughly 88-91%); alpha set to 10%
OPSA-AT SSCV stays low (e.g. 0.03-0.06 on CIFAR-100) while BETA-AT SSCV degrades badly (up to 0.90 under APGD100/Auto on CIFAR-100)
Perturbation budget r = 8/255 (0.03), L-infinity norm, attack step size 2/255
baselines_compared: Attacks: FGSM, PGD10, PGD40, BETA10, Square (1000 queries), APGD100, Auto100 (AutoAttack) -- OPSA is the paper's own attack
Defenses: FGSM-AT, PGD10-AT, TRADES (trade-off 5), MART (trade-off 5), BETA-AT, and OPSA-ST (soft-thresholding conformal-training ablation) -- OPSA-AT is the paper's own defense

CP methods referenced as related robustness baselines (not re-run in main tables): ARCP (Gendler et al. 2021), PRCP (Ghosh et al. 2023), RSCP+/RCT (Yan et al. 2024), VRCP (Jeary et al. 2024), AT-UR (Liu et al. 2024)
